political.s: Meta's PAC contributed approximately $5.5 million in the 2024 election cycle and spent $24.4 million on federal lobbying in 2024, making it one of the top corporate lobbying spenders. Contributions have historically split between both parties, with lobbying focused on AI regulation, data privacy, Section 230, and platform integrity. The company has recently aligned more openly with the Republican-led Trump administration through policy changes on content moderation and DEI.

charity.s: Charitable giving is primarily channeled through the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative (CZI), an LLC co-founded by Mark Zuckerberg and Priscilla Chan with a pledge to donate 99% of their Meta shares over their lifetimes. CZI has focused on biomedical research, education, and housing, with grants to university consortia including $800M–$1B to UC/Stanford biomedical research and $500M to Harvard's AI institute. In 2025, CZI refocused away from social justice causes toward AI-powered biomedical research, and Meta donated $50M to a Sacramento STEM campus.
charity.amt: Billions pledged via CZI over lifetime; ~$50M direct corporate donation noted in 2026
charity.focus: Biomedical research, AI, education, housing affordability

environment.s: Meta has maintained net-zero emissions in its global operations since 2020, reducing operational emissions by 94% from a 2017 baseline by matching 100% of electricity use with renewable energy. The company targets net-zero across its full value chain by 2030 and has signed contracts for over 11.7 GW of renewable energy. Critics note that location-based emissions grew significantly from ~5M to ~15M tons CO2 between 2019 and 2023, and that heavy reliance on carbon offsets and RECs raises greenwashing concerns amid massive AI-driven data center expansion.

labor.s: Meta has conducted multiple rounds of significant layoffs, including 21,000 cuts in 2022–2023 and a 10% workforce reduction (~8,000 jobs) announced in May 2026. Median total compensation declined from $417,400 in 2024 to $388,200 in 2025 amid stock award cuts, while executive bonuses were simultaneously raised to 200% of base salary. Employee morale has been widely reported as low, with internal surveillance software deployed on employee laptops and performance management practices drawing criticism.

dei.s: In January 2025, Meta officially terminated its DEI programs, disbanding its DEI team, ending its Diverse Slate Approach to hiring, eliminating equity and inclusion training, and sunsetting its supplier diversity program. Chief Diversity Officer Maxine Williams was moved to a new role focused on accessibility. The company cited shifting legal and political landscapes following recent Supreme Court decisions, drawing employee criticism internally and praise from Republican politicians.

animals.s: Meta is a software and social media technology company with no animal testing programs or products requiring animal testing. Animal welfare considerations are not applicable to its core business operations.

guns.s: Meta operates social media platforms and does not manufacture or sell firearms. The company has policies restricting certain gun-related marketplace listings and advertisements on its platforms, though enforcement has been inconsistent and periodically controversial.

privacy.s: Meta paid a record $5 billion FTC fine in 2019 for violating a 2012 privacy consent decree, and ongoing FTC proceedings continued through 2025. A four-year FTC investigation confirmed Meta tracks users across millions of websites and apps, with its business model fundamentally reliant on personal data collection for targeted advertising. Cambridge Analytica and other privacy scandals, plus a $190M board-level shareholder settlement in 2025 over data misuse, underscore a persistent pattern of privacy violations.

execPay.s: CEO Mark Zuckerberg takes a nominal $1 base salary but received $27.2 million in total FY2024 compensation (mostly personal security costs), while the median Meta employee earned approximately $379,000 in 2023 per SEC proxy filings. Other named executives each received $23–25 million in FY2024, and executive bonuses were raised to 200% of base salary in 2025 even as the broader workforce faced stock award cuts and layoffs. The official CEO-to-median-worker pay ratio is complex given Zuckerberg's atypical pay structure, but other top executives earn roughly 60–70x the median employee.
execPay.ratio: ~72:1 (other NEOs vs. median worker); CEO ratio atypical due to $1 base salary

sec.note: Pay ratio extracted from proxy filing — verify manually for accuracy
